summary: The January 2026 DoD AI Strategy established Agent Network as a Priority Strategic Program. CMMC enforcement is underway with fewer than 1% of 80,000 organizations certified. FedRAMP authorization is the mandatory gateway for cloud AI in federal systems — no major commercial AI platform has achieved FedRAMP High. AgentDash's air-gapped, multi-model architecture and certification timeline create a 24-month competitive moat that capital alone cannot compress.

workflows.name: SOC Alert Triage for Federal Networks
workflows.description: Agents correlate alerts from CDM sensors, CISA EINSTEIN, and agency SIEM systems across .gov and .mil networks. Suppress false positives, enrich with threat intelligence (CISA KEV, MITRE ATT&CK), and escalate genuine threats with evidence packets.
workflows.currentPain: Federal SOCs process thousands of daily alerts from CDM, EINSTEIN, and agency-specific sensors. Analyst shortage is acute — DHS CISA alone has 2,000+ unfilled cyber positions. Alert fatigue drives 30%+ annual turnover in government SOC roles.

workflows.name: CMMC Compliance Automation
workflows.description: Agents continuously assess contractor environments against CMMC Level 2 controls (110 NIST 800-171 practices), generate POA&Ms, collect evidence artifacts, and prepare for C3PAO assessments.
workflows.currentPain: 80,000 organizations need CMMC Level 2 certification. Fewer than 800 (<1%) have achieved it as of January 2026. Assessment costs $50-200K per organization. Manual compliance documentation takes 6-12 months.

workflows.name: Classified Environment Monitoring
workflows.description: Agents operate in air-gapped IL4/IL5/IL6 environments monitoring for insider threats, data exfiltration, and adversary persistence on classified networks — without any cloud connectivity.
workflows.currentPain: Classified networks cannot use cloud-based security tools. On-premise SIEM deployments are understaffed and under-automated. Nation-state adversaries specifically target classification boundaries.

workflows.name: Vulnerability Prioritization for Federal Systems
workflows.description: Agents cross-reference CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog with agency asset inventory, BOD 22-01 compliance deadlines, and actual exploit activity to prioritize patching.
workflows.currentPain: CISA BOD 22-01 mandates remediation timelines for KEV entries. Agencies struggle to meet deadlines across sprawling, heterogeneous infrastructure. Average federal agency has 100,000+ endpoints.

workflows.name: Incident Response for Government Systems
workflows.description: Agents execute NIST 800-61 incident response playbooks: contain, eradicate, recover, and generate CISA-formatted incident reports. Coordinate across multi-agency environments.
workflows.currentPain: Federal incident response requires coordination across CISA, agency SOC, IG, and sometimes FBI/Secret Service. Manual playbook execution takes hours during fast-moving incidents.

platformFit.rationale: AWS GovCloud with FedRAMP High for some services. But Bedrock Agents specifically may not have FedRAMP High authorization for all features. Cloud-only — cannot operate in air-gapped environments.
Open-source flexibility for custom deployments but no security certifications, no air-gap capability without significant custom engineering, and no compliance automation features.

businessOutcome: A federal civilian agency deploying agentic cybersecurity can expect: 80%+ alert noise reduction, 24/7 automated monitoring replacing business-hours SOC, CMMC compliance assessment time reduced from 12 months to 8 weeks for contractor oversight, continuous FISMA/NIST compliance posture, and $3-8M annual savings vs expanding human SOC staff in a market where GS-13/14 cyber analysts command $120-180K+ with full benefits.
examples: FY2026 NDAA directs DoD to develop AI/ML cybersecurity framework and incorporate it into CMMC — creating mandatory demand for AI-capable security platforms in the defense industrial base.
CMMC enforcement underway: fewer than 800 of 80,000 organizations certified as of January 2026. Massive backlog creates urgent demand for compliance automation.

No major commercial AI platform (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) has achieved FedRAMP High authorization for standard tiers — creating a massive barrier to entry that certification-first platforms can exploit.
Secureframe launched automated CMMC compliance with AI-generated SSPs (March 2026) — proving market appetite for AI-powered compliance automation in the defense industrial base.
obstacles: FedRAMP authorization requires 12-18 months and $1-3M investment — mandatory gateway, not optional
CMMC Level 2 certification for the platform itself if deployed in contractor environments
IL4/IL5/IL6 accreditation for classified environment deployment — additional 18-24 months
Government procurement cycles: 60-120 days via OTA, 6-18 months via traditional FAR-based contracts
ITAR/EAR restrictions on technology transfer if deploying to allied nation defense customers
Security clearance requirements for personnel supporting classified deployments

marketSizing.tam: $13.4B — DoD AI spending estimated annually, with cybersecurity as a top priority. Federal civilian cybersecurity spending exceeds $10B/year.
marketSizing.sam: $4.8B — AI-powered SOC automation, compliance automation, and threat detection for federal/state/local government and the 80,000-organization defense industrial base
marketSizing.som: $240M — Defense industrial base CMMC compliance automation + mid-tier federal civilian agency SOC automation where air-gapped operation is required
marketSizing.source: DoD AI budget analysis, CISA budget data, CMMC PMO contractor estimates

playbook.currentState: Government cybersecurity is at an inflection point. The FY2026 NDAA mandates a CMMC-for-AI framework. CISA is publishing AI cybersecurity guidance. FedRAMP is the mandatory gateway for cloud AI in federal systems — and no major AI platform has achieved FedRAMP High. Meanwhile, 80,000 defense contractors need CMMC certification with fewer than 1% certified. The talent shortage is structural: government pay scales (GS-13/14 = $120-180K fully loaded) cannot compete with private sector ($200-400K for senior cyber talent). The result: massive demand for AI-powered security with an enormous certification moat protecting early entrants. AgentDash's architecture — air-gapped, multi-model, self-improving — was designed for exactly this environment.

idealCustomerProfile.segment: Defense industrial base contractors (CMMC Level 2 required), mid-tier federal civilian agencies (CISA CDM participants), and intelligence community support contractors
idealCustomerProfile.size: Defense contractors: $50M-$5B revenue with CMMC obligations. Federal agencies: 1,000-50,000 endpoints. IC support: varies by contract scope.
idealCustomerProfile.painIntensity: Existential — CMMC non-compliance means loss of DoD contracts. Federal agencies face IG audit findings and FISMA scorecard pressure. IC support contractors face clearance revocation risk.
idealCustomerProfile.buyerTitle: CISO, VP Cybersecurity, or Director of Security Operations. For defense contractors: VP IT/Compliance. OTA authority often held by Chief Innovation Officer or CTO.
idealCustomerProfile.budget: $500K-$3M via OTA/SBIR/STTR for pilot. Production: $2-10M/year depending on scope. DIU has facilitated $4.9B+ in production contracts across 48 companies — proven pathway.

entryWedge.workflow: CMMC Compliance Automation — the most urgent, time-bounded pain point with 80,000 organizations needing certification
entryWedge.why: CMMC is a hard deadline with contract loss consequences. 80,000 organizations need it, fewer than 1% have it. The assessment process is manual, expensive ($50-200K), and slow (6-12 months). An AI-powered compliance automation tool that cuts this to 8 weeks has immediate, quantifiable value. Every defense contractor CIO is actively looking for this solution.
entryWedge.proofOfConcept: Deploy AgentDash agents to automate CMMC Level 2 self-assessment: agents scan the contractor's environment against all 110 NIST 800-171 practices, identify gaps, generate POA&Ms, collect evidence artifacts, and produce an assessment-ready package. Compare: time and cost vs manual assessment. Run with 3-5 defense contractors in a 60-day pilot.
entryWedge.timeToValue: 8-12 weeks. Week 1-2: Contractor environment discovery + NIST 800-171 control mapping. Week 3-4: Agent assessment + gap analysis. Week 5-8: Evidence collection automation + POA&M generation. Week 9-12: C3PAO assessment preparation package delivered.

successMetrics.target: 6-8 weeks with AI-powered evidence collection and gap analysis

successMetrics.baseline: $50-200K per organization for manual assessment preparation
successMetrics.target: $15-40K per organization with AI automation — 60-80% cost reduction

successMetrics.baseline: Point-in-time assessment that's stale within weeks
successMetrics.target: Real-time compliance dashboard with drift detection and auto-remediation recommendations

successMetrics.baseline: Business hours only at most government agencies
successMetrics.target: 24/7 AI-powered monitoring with human escalation for confirmed threats

successMetrics.metric: FedRAMP/CMMC certification timeline for AgentDash itself
successMetrics.baseline: N/A — no major AI platform has FedRAMP High
successMetrics.target: FedRAMP Moderate by month 12, FedRAMP High by month 18

riskAssessment.risk: FedRAMP authorization timeline delays market entry by 12-18 months
riskAssessment.mitigation: Pursue FedRAMP 20x (accelerated path). Start with OTA/SBIR contracts that don't require FedRAMP for initial deployments. On-premise deployment avoids FedRAMP requirement entirely for contractor environments. DIU pathway provides non-competitive follow-on production contracts.

riskAssessment.risk: Platform classified as 'AI/ML technology' under new NDAA framework, requiring additional certification beyond CMMC
riskAssessment.mitigation: Engage with CDAO early to shape the framework. Position AgentDash as security infrastructure (like a SIEM) rather than an AI decision-making system. Document compliance with NIST AI RMF from day one.

riskAssessment.risk: Competition from Palantir/Anduril entering the agentic security space
riskAssessment.mitigation: Palantir is a data fusion platform, not an agent runtime. Anduril is autonomous systems, not enterprise security. Neither offers air-gapped multi-model agent orchestration. Position AgentDash as the orchestration layer BENEATH Palantir AIP or Anduril Lattice — complementary, not competitive.

riskAssessment.risk: Government shutdown or continuing resolution freezes new contract awards
riskAssessment.mitigation: Target multi-year OTA contracts that survive CRs. Build pipeline across both DoD and civilian agencies to diversify funding risk. SBIR/STTR funding is typically protected during shutdowns.

regulatoryConsiderations: FY2026 NDAA: Directs DoD to develop AI/ML cybersecurity framework and incorporate into DFARS and CMMC — creating mandatory demand for AI-capable security in the defense industrial base.
FedRAMP: Mandatory for cloud-hosted AI in federal systems. No major AI platform has FedRAMP High. This is a 12-18 month certification moat. FedRAMP 20x may accelerate the path.
CMMC Level 2: 110 NIST 800-171 practices required for all defense contractors handling CUI. Enforcement is underway with 80,000 organizations in scope.
CISA AI Cybersecurity Guidance: Federal agencies receiving guidance on deploying AI in security operations — creating a framework for responsible adoption.
EO 14110 (Safe, Secure, Trustworthy AI): Requires Chief AI Officers and AI impact assessments at federal agencies. Security AI deployments must comply.
IL4/IL5/IL6 Classification Levels: Air-gapped environments require specific accreditation. IL5 covers controlled unclassified. IL6 covers classified up to SECRET.
playbook.competitiveLandscape: Government cybersecurity AI is dominated by defense primes and GovTech specialists: (1) Palantir ($30.5B valuation) — data fusion and decision support via AIP. Consumes agents but doesn't build them. AgentDash positions beneath Palantir. (2) Anduril ($30.5B valuation) — autonomous systems and Lattice OS. Focused on physical domain autonomy, not enterprise cyber. (3) SAIC/Booz Allen/Leidos — traditional defense IT services adding AI. Human-staffed, not agentic. Slow to innovate. (4) CrowdStrike GovCloud — Charlotte AI has government customers but cloud-only with no air-gap. (5) Trellix (McAfee/FireEye) — legacy government installed base but aging platform. (6) Secureframe — emerging CMMC compliance automation (launched March 2026) but not agentic. NONE offer air-gapped multi-model agent orchestration with self-improving capabilities. The certification moat (FedRAMP 12-18mo, CMMC 6-12mo, IL5 18-24mo) means a competitor starting today cannot serve classified environments until 2028-2029.
playbook.pricingModel: Government pricing follows specific models: (1) OTA Pilot: $500K-$2M via DIU Commercial Solutions Opening or service-specific OTA. Non-competitive follow-on production contracts up to $100M. (2) SBIR/STTR: Phase I $250K, Phase II $750K-$1.5M. Provides non-dilutive R&D funding. (3) Production contracts: Per-endpoint pricing ($5-15/endpoint/month) or per-user pricing for CMMC compliance ($100-500/contractor organization/month). (4) Platform licensing: $50K-500K/year depending on classification level and deployment scope. (5) Sovereign tier: Revenue-share model where AgentDash earns based on contract value enabled by its platform.

deploymentTimeline.milestone: Contract awarded via DIU CSO or service OTA. $500K-$2M initial funding secured.
Security documentation (SSP, POAM, SAR) prepared. Penetration testing completed. ATO package submitted.
Agents deployed on .gov network segment. SIEM API integration. CDM sensor correlation. Shadow mode activated.
False positive suppression rate measured. Detection accuracy validated against MITRE ATT&CK evaluations. Compliance evidence quality reviewed.
Full ATO granted. Agents in autonomous triage mode. Continuous monitoring dashboards operational.
IL5/IL6 accreditation. Air-gapped deployment. Cross-domain solution integration. Expanded to classified network monitoring.

buyerJourney.action: Agency CISO reads FY2026 NDAA AI/ML cybersecurity framework mandate. Or: defense contractor CEO realizes CMMC certification is required for contract renewal and current timeline is 12+ months.
CISO team searches for AI-capable security platforms. Key filter: can it operate in our classification environment? Does it have FedRAMP/ATO? Can it help with CMMC?
Engage via DIU Commercial Solutions Opening, service-specific OTA, or SBIR/STTR. 60-120 day procurement timeline — 5-10x faster than traditional FAR.
Deploy on unclassified network segment. Validate detection accuracy, compliance evidence quality, and integration with existing security stack.
buyerJourney.stakeholder: SOC Manager, IT Security, C3PAO (for CMMC)

buyerJourney.action: Present pilot results to agency leadership. Build case for production ATO and multi-year funding. DIU pathway: non-competitive follow-on production contract.
Pursue IL5/IL6 accreditation for classified network monitoring. This is the 18-24 month moat — once achieved, competitors face the same timeline.
